source_metadata.title_raw: Peter J. Nolan  - A-18-274 - February 22, 2019 - Palm Springs

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 19, 2019

Peter J. Nolan
SBEMP, LLP
1800 E. Tahquitz Canyon Way
Palm Springs, California 92262

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-274

Dear Mr. Nolan:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Needles City Councilmembers
Zachery Longacre and Timothy Terral regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions. Also note that we
are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

(1) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in governmental
decisions relating to a proposed ordinance that would update the Needles Municipal Code's
enforcement and penalty provisions applicable to cannabis businesses permitted to do business
within the City (the "Ordinance") given that he recently resigned his position with a cannabis
dispensary permitted by the City, he received more than $500 in income from that dispensary in the
last 12 months, and the City recently revoked that dispensary's operating permit for insufficient
record keeping?

(2) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to
a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the operating permit of the dispensary that formerly
employed him?

(3) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions relating to the
Ordinance given that his wife has been offered a position with a cannabis dispensary permitted by
the City?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-274
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

(1) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating
to the Ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would have a material financial
effect on the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed him.

(2) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating
to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of that dispensary's operating permit.

(3) No. The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions
relating to the Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a
material financial effect on his interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family
members.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Your firm serves as the City Attorney for the City of Needles, and you are the authorized
representative of newly elected Needles City Councilmembers Zachary Longacre and Timothy
Terral. You provided additional facts relating to the questions presented on January 29, 2019.

Prior to the recent election of Councilmember Longacre and Councilmember Terral to the
Needles City Council, the City authorized the operation of City-permitted cannabis businesses
within the City in accordance with State law. There are currently 18 permitted cannabis businesses
of various types operating within the City, and four of those businesses are cannabis dispensaries.
The City Council will soon consider a proposed ordinance to update the Needles Municipal Code's
enforcement and penalty provisions applicable to cannabis businesses within the City (the
"Ordinance").

In his private capacity, Councilmember Longacre was an employee of a cannabis dispensary
permitted to do business within the City until he recently resigned his position with that dispensary.
Although the City has revoked that dispensary's operating permit due to insufficient record
keeping, Councilmember Longacre has received more than $500 in income from that dispensary in
the last 12 months. The Needles Municipal Code's existing enforcement and penalty provisions
applicable to cannabis businesses authorize an appeal of the City's revocation or suspension of a
cannabis business's operating permit before the City Council. (See Section 12A-21 of the Needles
Municipal Code.) If adopted by the City Council, the Ordinance would only apply prospectively.

A friend of Councilmember Terral's wife owns one of the four cannabis dispensaries
currently permitted to operate within the City, and this individual has offered Mrs. Terral a position
with that dispensary. Mrs. Terral has not accepted the position to date, and is holding off on
proceeding for further direction from your firm. You state that you are not aware of any actions by
Mrs. Terral to pursue employment in the cannabis industry aside from her discussing potential
employment with her friend who owns the dispensary.

File No. A-18-274
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include
all of the following:

• A business interest in any business entity in which the official has an investment of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is an employee or holds a position of
management (Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)

• A source of income interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official in
the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• A gift interest in any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more to the official in the 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of
immediate family members.

With respect to decisions relating to the Ordinance, and those relating to the potential appeal
of the City's revocation of the operating permit of the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed
him, Councilmember Longacre has a source of income interest in that dispensary because he has
received more than $500 in income from that dispensary in the last 12 months, and an interest in his
personal finances and those of immediate family members. Councilmember Longacre does not have
a business interest in that dispensary because he is no longer employed by it, and the facts presented
provide no indication he has any investment in it.

Councilmember Terral's interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family
members is also implicated by decisions relating to the Ordinance. The Act defines "immediate
family" as the spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.) A friend of Councilmember Terral's
wife, who owns one of the four cannabis dispensaries permitted to operate within the City, has
offered Mrs. Terral a position with that dispensary. No other financial interest identified in Section
87103 is implicated by an official's spouse's mere consideration of whether to accept an
employment offer. Therefore, Councilmember Terral's sole interest at issue with respect to

File No. A-18-274
Page No. 4

decisions relating to the Ordinance is his interest in his personal finances and those of immediate
family members.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest.

With respect to any decisions relating to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the
operating permit for the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre, his
source of income interest in that business would be a named party in or the subject of those
decisions. Therefore, Councilmember Longacre's source of income interest in that dispensary is
explicitly involved in those decisions, and those decisions' effect on that interest is presumed
reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect is
reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical
or theoretical."

Neither Councilmember Longacre's source of income interest in the dispensary that
formerly employed him, nor his interest in his personal finances, nor Councilmember Terral's
interest in his personal finances is explicitly involved in decisions relating to the Ordinance.
Therefore, the effect of those decisions on each of those interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Councilmember Longacre

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's interest in a source of income. Subdivision (a)(4) of that regulation, however, provides
that if the official's income is from a business entity's sale of good and services, materiality is
determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth the materiality standards applicable to
an official's interest in a business entity.

Councilmember Longacre's income from the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed
him appears to be from that business's sale of goods or services. Therefore, we apply Regulation
18702.1 to determine whether the decisions relating to the Ordinance would have a disqualifying
effect on the Councilmember's source of income interest in that business.

² The Act's definition of "income" includes "any community property interest in the income of a spouse."
(Section 82030(a).) Therefore, we note that Councilmember Terral would also have an interest in any source of income
to his wife in which his community property interest is $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue.

File No. A-18-274
Page No. 5

As pertinent to decisions relating to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the
operating permit for the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre,
Regulation 18702.1(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business is
material if the business:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government
action concerning the business entity;

[¶]…[¶]

(6) Is the subject of any inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the
regulatory authority of the official's agency; or

(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the
effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has
an interest.

The City's existing enforcement and penalty provisions applicable to cannabis businesses
provide an appeal process for a cannabis business that has its operating permit revoked or
suspended by the City. (See Section 12A-21 of the Needles Municipal Code.) It is unclear from the
facts presented whether the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre
will appeal the City's revocation of its operating permit. However, if the dispensary appeals, the
Act would prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to the appeal
because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on
his source of income interest in the dispensary under Regulations 18702.1(a)(1), (6), and (7).

As pertinent to decisions relating to the Ordinance, Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a
decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a private-held
business is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of that
business. Examples of decisions that may contribute to a change in the value of a business include
decisions that authorize, prohibit, regulate, or establish conditions for an activity in which the
business is engaged or increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business is engaged. (Regulation 18702.1(b)(1) and (2).)

The facts presented are silent as to whether the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed
Councilmember Longacre has gone out of business permanently, may reopen, or continues to do
business under a different business model since the City's revocation of its operating permit.
Because we do not have sufficient facts about that dispensary to conclusively determine whether the
decisions relating to the Ordinance would have a disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's
source of income interest in the dispensary, we are only able to provide general advice at this time.
Accordingly, the Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to
the Ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that they will have a material financial effect on one or
more of his interests, including his source of income interest in the cannabis dispensary that
formerly employed him.

File No. A-18-274
Page No. 6

Councilmember Terral

Regulation 18702.5 provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in his personal finances or those of immediate family members is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision.

The facts presented provide no indication that: Councilmember Terral or any of his immediate family members has a present-day financial interest in any cannabis business within the City; the decisions relating to the Ordinance would have any financial effect on Councilmember Terral's personal finances or those of his immediate family members; or the employment offer to Councilmember Terral's wife is contingent upon any condition. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit him from taking part in those decisions because it is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to the Ordinance will result in a measurable financial benefit or loss to Councilmember Terral or any of his immediate family members based on the facts presented.³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By:    Matthew F. Christy
       Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

³ We note, however, that if there is any indication that the employment offer is at all contingent upon the outcome of decisions relating to the Ordinance, you should seek additional advice because those decisions may have a disqualifying effect on Councilmember Terral's interest in his personal finances. We also note that if Councilmember Terral's wife accepts the dispensary owner's employment offer, and if Councilmember Terral receives $500 or more in income from the dispensary pursuant to his community property interest in his wife's income, Councilmember Terral would have a source of income interest in the dispensary. You should also seek further advice if Councilmember Terral obtains a source of income interest in the dispensary and if there is any indication that a decision before the City Council may have a disqualifying effect on that interest.

(1) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in governmental

(2) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to

(3) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions relating to the

the City?
sections.conclusion:
(1) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating 
to the Ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would have a material financial 
effect on the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed him. 
(2) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating 
to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of that dispensary's operating permit. 
(3) No. The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a 
material financial effect on his interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family 
members.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect 
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an 
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include 
all of the following: 
• 
A business interest in any business entity in which the official has an investment of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is an employee or holds a position of 
management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
A source of income interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official in 
the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
A gift interest in any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more to the official in the 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of 
immediate family members. 
With respect to decisions relating to the Ordinance, and those relating to the potential appeal 
of the City's revocation of the operating permit of the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed 
him, Councilmember Longacre has a source of income interest in that dispensary because he has 
received more than $500 in income from that dispensary in the last 12 months, and an interest in his 
personal finances and those of immediate family members. founcilmember Longacre does not have 
a business interest in that dispensary because he is no longer employed by it, and the facts presented 
provide no indication he has any investment in it. 
Councilmember Terral's interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family 
members is also implicated by decisions relating to the Ordinance. The Act defines "immediate 
family" as the spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.) A friend of Councilmember Terral's 
wife, who owns one of the four cannabis dispensaries permitted to operate within the City, has 
offered Mrs. Terral a position with that dispensary. No other financial interest identified in Section 
87103 is implicated by an official's spouse's mere consideration of whether to accept an 
employment offer. Therefore, Councilmember Terral's sole interest at issue with respect to  
decisions relating to the Ordinance is his interest in his personal finances and those of immediate 
family members. 2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 
With respect to any decisions relating to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the 
operating permit for the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre, his 
source of income interest in that business would be a named party in or the subject of those 
decisions. Therefore, Councilmember Longacre's source of income interest in that dispensary is 
explicitly involved in those decisions, and those decisions' effect on that interest is presumed 
reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect 
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." 
Neither Councilmember Longacre's source of income interest in the dispensary that 
formerly employed him, nor his interest in his personal finances, nor Councilmember Terral's 
interest in his personal finances is explicitly involved in decisions relating to the Ordinance. 
Therefore, the effect of those decisions on each of those interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can 
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Councilmember Longacre 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's interest in a source of income. Subdivision (a)(4) of that regulation, however, provides 
that if the official's income is from a business entity's sale of good and services, materiality is 
determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth the materiality standards applicable to 
an official's interest in a business entity. 
Councilmember Longacre's income from the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed 
him appears to be from that business's sale of goods or services. Therefore, we apply Regulation 
18702.1 to determine whether the decisions relating to the Ordinance would have a disqualifying 
effect on the Councilmember' s source of income interest in that business. 
2 The Act's definition of"income" includes "any community property interest in the income ofa spouse." 
(Section 82030(a).) Therefore, we note that Councilmember Terral would also have an interest in any source of income 
to his wife in which his community property interest is $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue.  
As pertinent to decisions relating to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the 
operating permit for the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre, 
Regulation 18702.l(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business is 
material if the business: 
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be 
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government 
action concerning the business entity; 
(6) Is the subject of any inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the 
regulatory authority of the official's agency; or 
(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the 
effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has 
an interest. 
The City's existing enforcement and penalty provisions applicable to cannabis businesses 
provide an appeal process for a cannabis business that has its operating permit revoked or 
suspended by the City. (See Section 12A-21 of the Needles Municipal Code.) It is unclear from the 
facts presented whether the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed Councilmember Longacre 
will appeal the City's revocation of its operating permit. However, if the dispensary appeals, the 
Act would prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to the appeal 
because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on 
his source of income interest in the dispensary under Regulations 18702.l(a.)(1), (6), and (7). 
As pertinent to decisions relating to the Ordinance, Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that a 
decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in a privately-held 
business is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the value of that 
business. Examples of decisions that may contribute to a change in the value of a business include 
decisions that authorize, prohibit, regulate, or establish conditions for an activity in which the 
business is engaged or increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business is engaged. (Regulation 18702.l(b)(l) and (2).) 
The facts presented are silent as to whether the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed 
Councilmember Longacre has gone out of business permanently, may reopen, or continues to do 
business under a different business model since the City's revocation of its operating permit. 
Because we do not have sufficient facts about that dispensary to conclusively determine whether the 
decisions relating to the Ordinance would have a disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's 
source of income interest in the dispensary, we are only able to provide general advice at this time. 
Accordingly, the Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to 
the Ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that they will have a material financial effect on one or 
more of his interests, including his source of income interest in the cannabis dispensary that 
formerly employed him. 


Councilmember Terral  
Regulation 18702.5 provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in his personal 
finances or those of immediate family members is material if the official or the official's immediate 
family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. 
The facts presented provide no indication that: Councilmember Terral or any of his 
immediate family members has a present-day financial interest in any cannabis business within the 
City; the decisions relating to the Ordinance would have any financial effect on Councilmember 
Terral's personal finances or those of his immediate family members; or the employment offer to 
Councilmember Terral's wife is contingent upon any condition. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit 
him from taking part in those decisions because it is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions 
relating to the Ordinance will result in a measurable financial benefit or loss to Councilmember 
Terral or any of his immediate family members based on the facts presented.3

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in governmental 
decisions relating to a proposed ordinance that would update the Needles Municipal Code's 
enforcement and penalty provisions applicable to cannabis businesses permitted to do business 
within the City (the "Ordinance") given that he recently resigned his position with a cannabis 
dispensary permitted by the City, he received more than $500 in income from that dispensary in the 
last 12 months, and the City recently revoked that dispensary's operating permit for insufficient 
record keeping? 
(2) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating to 
a potential appeal of the City's revocation of the operating permit of the dispensary that formerly 
employed him? 
(3) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions relating to the 
Ordinance given that his wife has been offered a position with a cannabis dispensary permitted by 
the City?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating 
to the Ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would have a material financial 
effect on the cannabis dispensary that formerly employed him. 
(2) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Longacre from taking part in decisions relating 
to a potential appeal of the City's revocation of that dispensary's operating permit. 
(3) No. The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Terral from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Ordinance because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions will have a 
material financial effect on his interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family 
members.
